name: simultaneous-velocity-step-preserves-wavelength
statement: A simultaneous, spatially-uniform change in velocity across a stream of moving bodies preserves their spatial separation but alters their temporal arrival frequency, whereas a localized boundary change in velocity preserves their temporal frequency but alters their spatial separation.
why_it_bites: It punishes the intuitive but incorrect assumption that the time interval between successive arrivals remains invariant during a global speed change, or that the spatial gap must scale with the speed change in all scenarios.
trap_recipe: Ask for the new arrival interval after a global, simultaneous speed reduction. The trap answer is the original time interval (assuming frequency conservation, as is typical in wave-refraction boundary problems) or an inverse scaling of the spatial separation.
example_sketch: Fleet delivery drones fly in a line separated by 60 seconds at a speed of 20 m/s. A sudden GPS software update simultaneously reduces the speed of all drones to 15 m/s to conserve power. A receiver station now measures the time interval between drone arrivals.

name: radial-coordinate-curvature-conflation
statement: In curvilinear motion, the radius of curvature governing normal acceleration is distinct from the radial coordinate distance to the origin, coinciding only for circular paths centered at that origin.
why_it_bites: Punishes the over-generalization that any curved path's centripetal acceleration can be computed using the distance $r$ from a convenient reference origin as the radius of curvature.
trap_recipe: Describe a particle on a spiral or parabolic path and ask for its acceleration. Offer choices that use the instantaneous distance $r$ from the origin instead of the proper osculating circle radius.
example_sketch: A bead slides along a parabolic wire $y = x^2$ at constant speed. At a given point, a student incorrectly calculates its normal acceleration by dividing the square of its speed by its straight-line distance to the origin.

name: spatial-vs-temporal-midpoint-of-deceleration
statement: For an object undergoing uniform acceleration, its speed at the spatial midpoint of an interval is the root-mean-square of the boundary speeds, not their arithmetic mean.
why_it_bites: It punishes the intuitive but incorrect assumption that because deceleration is constant, the velocity decreases linearly with distance rather than with time.
trap_recipe: Specify an object decelerating uniformly between two benchmarks, state the speeds at these benchmarks, and ask for the speed at the exact physical midpoint, offering the simple average of the two speeds as a highly attractive distractor.
example_sketch: A train braking with a constant deceleration slows from $40\text{ m/s}$ to $20\text{ m/s}$ over a straight bridge. A student calculates its speed exactly halfway across the bridge to be $30\text{ m/s}$, failing to realize that the quadratic relationship between velocity and position places the actual speed at approximately $31.6\text{ m/s}$.

name: drifting-frame-invariant-geometry
statement: When a group of objects is released sequentially into a uniformly moving medium, their geometric pattern remains rigid and invariant in the co-moving frame of the medium, while appearing translated or sheared in the stationary frame.
why_it_bites: It exploits the tendency of students to perform tedious kinematic calculations for each individual object in the stationary ground frame, leading to algebraic errors, rather than switching to the frame of the moving fluid where the geometry is trivial.
trap_recipe: Describe a symmetric geometric shape formed by dropping markers into a moving fluid at specified times, and ask what shape they form in the ground frame, providing options where the shape is distorted into complex curves rather than simple Galilean translations.
example_sketch: A drone drops floating beacons at regular intervals into a steadily flowing river such that they would form a perfect circle if the water were static. In the stationary ground frame, the resulting pattern of beacons remains a perfect circle of the same size, merely translated downstream as a rigid body.

name: quadratic-vs-linear-rate-reversal
statement: When an object's position is governed by a combination of a constant-rate erosion/melting process (linear in time) and a constant-acceleration mechanical push (quadratic in time) acting in opposite directions, the object reverses its direction of motion, meaning total distance traveled is strictly greater than net displacement.
why_it_bites: Students often calculate the net change in position (displacement) over a time interval and equate it to distance, failing to check if the velocity of the boundary crossed zero during that interval.
trap_recipe: Set up a scenario where an accelerating mechanical pusher advances an object while it is simultaneously melting at a constant linear rate. Ask for the total distance traveled by the interface over an interval containing the turnaround point, and provide the net displacement as the primary distractor.
example_sketch: A block of dry ice is pushed against a wall by a piston accelerating at $2\text{ mm/s}^2$ while sublimation continuously reduces its length at a constant rate of $4\text{ mm/s}$. Over the first $6\text{ s}$, the block's front face first retreats due to sublimation and then advances due to the piston's acceleration; finding the total distance traveled requires locating the turnaround point at $2\text{ s}$.

name: band-limited-dispersion-overlap
statement: A localized group of particles traveling with a finite spread of velocities disperses over time, and periodic groups released at regular intervals merge into a continuous, indistinguishable flow when the spatial spread of one group equals the spatial gap to the next.
why_it_bites: Students often confuse the internal dispersion of a single group with the relative dispersion between successive groups, attempting to find when a single group's front catches its own tail rather than when a faster group catches the slower preceding group.
trap_recipe: Specify a range of constant velocities for vehicles released during green light phases of a known duration, and ask for the distance down the road where the traffic flow becomes uniform, offering distractors calculated by setting the dispersion of a single group equal to the distance between traffic lights.
example_sketch: A hopper drops batches of marbles onto a slide every $5\text{ s}$, with individual marble speeds ranging from $2\text{ m/s}$ to $3\text{ m/s}$. To find where the batches merge into a continuous stream, one must calculate the distance where the fastest marble of a later batch catches up to the slowest marble of the immediately preceding batch.

name: origin-relative-radial-transverse-decomposition
statement: Decomposing velocity and acceleration into radial and transverse components relative to a fixed origin (rather than the trajectory's center of curvature) leads to non-intuitive time-dependence because the reference axes continuously rotate as the particle moves.
why_it_bites: Students routinely conflate 'radial and transverse' (defined relative to a position vector from an arbitrary origin) with 'tangential and normal' (defined relative to the local velocity vector), leading them to falsely assume radial acceleration is zero or that transverse velocity behaves like tangential velocity.
trap_recipe: Ask for the qualitative behavior of the radial or transverse component of acceleration for a standard projectile thrown horizontally from a tower, and provide options that match the tangential/normal components instead.
example_sketch: A particle is launched horizontally from a high cliff under constant gravity. A student must determine how the radial component of its acceleration relative to the launch point behaves over time. While the total acceleration vector is constant, its projection onto the shifting radial unit vector first decreases to a minimum and then asymptotically increases back toward the full value of gravity.
signals: origin of the coordinate system at the point of projection
components along and perpendicular to a position vector

name: kinematic-differential-state-switching
statement: When tracking the separation between two independently moving bodies that start and stop at different times, the piecewise transitions between quadratic and linear regions of the separation-time graph pinpoint the exact moments of individual state changes.
why_it_bites: It punishes the instinct to write a single continuous equation of motion across the entire time domain, forcing the solver to recognize that a linear separation graph implies identical velocities (or one stationary and the other moving linearly) while a quadratic separation graph implies relative acceleration.
trap_recipe: Present a graph of the distance between two dropped objects where the curve becomes linear and then quadratic again. Ask for the height of the drop, creating a distractor that assumes the linear portion corresponds to terminal velocity rather than one of the objects having already landed and stopped.
example_sketch: Two identical landing pods are dropped sequentially from a hovering drone. The distance between them is tracked on a graph, showing a parabolic rise, a linear phase, and a final parabolic decrease. The solver must deduce the time delay between drops and the height of the drone purely from the transition times on the graph.
signals: separation between the stones is plotted against time

name: rigid-linkage-velocity-projection
statement: The velocity of a point constrained to maintain fixed distances from two moving reference points is determined by projecting the reference velocities onto the instantaneous connecting lines, ensuring the rate of change of the link lengths is zero.
why_it_bites: It punishes laborious coordinate differentiation (which leads to algebraic nightmares) by showing that relative velocity along a rigid link must be zero, reducing a 2D motion problem to a system of simple 1D projections.
trap_recipe: Position two moving platforms on a coordinate axis and place a rolling cylinder or pinned joint connected to both by rigid rods of different lengths. Ask for the velocity of the joint at a specific asymmetric angle, offering incorrect choices obtained by simply averaging the platform velocities or ignoring the angular projections.
example_sketch: Two ships sail along perpendicular channels with known constant speeds. A floating buoy is tethered to both ships with inextensible ropes of fixed, unequal lengths. At a specific moment when the ropes make known angles with the channels, the velocity of the buoy must be calculated.
signals: fixed distance from two moving particles

name: bottleneck-separation-dip
statement: When a convoy of identical particles traveling with a constant temporal headway passes through a localized speed-reducing bottleneck, the graph of their mutual separation versus time displays a characteristic symmetric profile whose flat minimum corresponds to both particles being inside the bottleneck.
why_it_bites: Solvers often struggle to translate temporal spacing into spatial separation, failing to realize that the spatial separation changes only when one particle is inside the bottleneck and the other is outside, and remains constant when both are in the same medium.
trap_recipe: Provide a separation-time graph for two cars crossing a speed-bump zone. Ask for the length of the slow zone. Create a distractor that calculates the zone length by multiplying the maximum separation by the total duration of the dip, neglecting that the transition slope depends on the difference in speeds.
example_sketch: Two conveyor belts of different speeds are joined by a short transition ramp. Two boxes are placed on the first belt separated by a known time delay. A graph plots their physical separation as they pass onto the second belt, and the student must find the speed of the second belt from the plateau value of the separation.
signals: separation between the cars varies with time
speed of the cars on the road
speed of the cars on the bridge

name: tangential-alignment-extremum
statement: The angular elevation of an object moving along a continuous curve, viewed from a fixed tracking point, reaches a local extremum when the object's velocity vector points directly along or away from the line of sight of the observer.
why_it_bites: Solvers try to find the derivative of the arctangent function of the trajectory coordinates, leading to messy calculus, instead of using the geometric condition that the angular velocity vanishes when the velocity vector is parallel to the position vector.
trap_recipe: A plane flies horizontally at a constant speed, then enters a loop. A radar tracks its elevation angle. Ask for the wind speed or plane speed when the radar's elevation angle is at its maximum, providing options that assume the maximum angle corresponds to the maximum physical height of the loop.
example_sketch: A rocket ascends vertically, then starts curved horizontal flight due to steering. A tracking station on the ground measures the elevation angle, which peaks at a certain value before decreasing. At this peak, the rocket's velocity vector is aligned with the line of sight, allowing its horizontal component to be calculated if the vertical ascent rate is known.
signals: angle of elevation acquires its maximum value

name: smoke-trail-wind-gauge
statement: The direction of a continuous trail of exhaust or smoke emitted by a moving body aligns with the vector difference between the ambient wind velocity and the body's velocity.
why_it_bites: Students often treat smoke trails as velocity vectors pointing in the direction of the wind itself, or they incorrectly add the source velocity to the trail direction, neglecting that the trail is a timeline of abandoned stationary points drifting with the wind.
trap_recipe: Provide the speed of a vehicle and the compass direction of its exhaust trail, and ask for the wind vector; include distractor choices where the trail angle is directly treated as the wind angle or vector-added as a velocity.
example_sketch: An airplane flying East at 200 km/h leaves a vapor trail pointing North-West. A second plane flying North at 200 km/h leaves a vapor trail pointing West. Determine the speed and direction of the wind at this altitude.

name: radial-projection-closest-approach
statement: The rate of change of the distance between a body moving in a straight line and a fixed target is the projection of the velocity vector onto the line of sight, which is zero at the point of closest approach.
why_it_bites: Students confuse the rate of approach (radial velocity) with the absolute speed of the object, leading them to either assume constant radial speed or fail to relate the geometry of the trajectory to the rate's time-dependence.
trap_recipe: Provide the rate of distance decrease at one instant and the constant absolute speed of the object, then ask for the closest distance of approach, tempting students to use a simple linear distance formula $d = v_{radial} t$.
example_sketch: A meteor moves through space at a constant velocity. At $t = 0$, its distance to a deep-space sensor is decreasing at $30\text{ km/s}$, and $10\text{ s}$ later it reaches its closest point to the sensor, where its actual speed is measured to be $50\text{ km/s}$. Find the minimum distance between the meteor and the sensor.

name: collinear-kinematic-interferometry
statement: If multiple moving points are constrained to remain collinear at all times, their positions, velocities, and accelerations must satisfy a weighted linear interpolation formula dictated solely by their spatial spacing.
why_it_bites: Students attempt to solve the system dynamically using forces and masses, missing the purely kinematic constraint that couples the derivatives of the coordinates.
trap_recipe: Describe three collinear particles where the outer two undergo distinct, independent accelerations, and ask for the required acceleration of the middle particle. Provide incorrect answers based on simple averaging that neglect asymmetric spacing.
example_sketch: Three quadcopters fly in a vertical plane such that they are always in a straight line, with the middle drone twice as far from the left drone as it is from the right drone. If the left drone rises with constant velocity $v$ and the right drone rises from rest with constant acceleration $a$, find the upward acceleration of the middle drone as a function of time.

name: steady-state-plume-mass
statement: The total instantaneous mass of a continuous, non-interacting stream of particles in flight is the product of the mass flow rate at the nozzle and the time-of-flight of an individual particle.
why_it_bites: Students attempt to perform a complex line integral of the mass density along the curved trajectory of the stream, struggling with how gravity or drag stretches the stream and changes its local cross-sectional area.
trap_recipe: Provide the cross-sectional area of a nozzle, the fluid density, and the projectile parameters of a jet, and ask for the mass of the fluid in mid-air. Include distractors that require integrating the parabolic arc length or calculating a varying stream thickness.
example_sketch: A firefighter aims a hose at a window at height $h$ with a nozzle velocity $u$ at an angle $\theta$. If the water exits the nozzle with a cross-sectional area $A$ and density $\rho$ and lands directly on the sill, find the mass of the water currently in the air.
signals: mass of water in the stream

name: velocity-space-rectilinear-path
statement: To transition between two velocity vectors of equal magnitude in the minimum possible time under a constraint on maximum acceleration magnitude, the optimal path in velocity space is a straight line, corresponding to a constant physical acceleration vector.
why_it_bites: Solvers intuitively assume that the physical trajectory must be a symmetric circular arc or that the acceleration vector should rotate to match the velocity's orientation, which yields a longer transition time.
trap_recipe: Ask for the minimum time to execute a ninety-degree turn at constant speed with a maximum thruster acceleration, and include the time calculated using a constant-radius circular path as a tempting wrong choice.
example_sketch: A drone flying at a speed of ten meters per second due north must change its velocity to ten meters per second due east using a thruster with a maximum acceleration of two meters per second squared. Instead of executing a circular turn of constant radius, the drone minimizes turning time by applying its maximum acceleration in a constant direction forty-five degrees south of east. This results in a parabolic physical path rather than a circular one.

name: kinematic-projection-asymmetry
statement: When a constraint is governed by an inextensible connector, the projection of the velocity of the moving body along the line of the connector must equal the velocity of the connector itself, rather than projecting the connector's velocity onto the coordinate axes of the body's motion.
why_it_bites: It punishes the common mistake of resolving the velocity vector of the moving body along the string's direction by multiplying by the cosine of the angle, which incorrectly yields a speed slower than the pull speed rather than faster.
trap_recipe: Design a system where a slider is pulled along a track by a rope passing over a pulley, and make the incorrect answer the product of the rope's speed and the cosine of the angle, while the correct answer divides by the cosine.
example_sketch: A collar is free to slide along a vertical guide rod and is connected to a cable that is wound in over a fixed pulley at a constant speed. When the cable makes a sixty-degree angle with the vertical guide, the instantaneous speed of the collar is twice the speed of the cable, not half of it. This occurs because the constraint dictates that the projection of the collar's velocity along the cable must equal the cable's speed.

name: non-orthogonal-shadow-projection
statement: The instantaneous horizontal velocity of a shadow cast on a flat plane by an object in two-dimensional motion under a tilted parallel light source is a linear combination of both the horizontal and vertical velocity components of the object.
why_it_bites: Solvers frequently assume that the shadow of an object on a horizontal floor moves at the exact same horizontal speed as the object itself, neglecting how vertical displacement shifts the shadow when the light rays are non-vertical.
trap_recipe: Ask for the shadow speed of a projectile at its peak when the sun is at a low angle in the sky, and list the projectile's horizontal velocity component as a high-confidence distractor.
example_sketch: A ball is launched in a vertical parabolic arc while the sun is low on the horizon, shining at an angle of thirty degrees to the ground. The shadow of the ball on the horizontal field accelerates and decelerates because the shadow's speed is influenced by the ball's vertical rise and fall. Even as the ball's actual horizontal speed remains constant, its shadow's speed varies continuously with the vertical velocity component.
signals: sun is shining at an angle

name: pursuit-centripetal-acceleration
statement: For a pursuer moving at constant speed that always steers directly toward a moving target, the instantaneous acceleration is purely centripetal and is governed by the angular velocity of the line of sight between the two bodies.
why_it_bites: Because the pursuer moves at a constant speed, students often mistakenly believe its acceleration is zero, or they fail to project the target's velocity perpendicular to the line of sight to find the correct instantaneous radius of curvature.
trap_recipe: Provide the speed of the pursuer, the velocity of the target, and the instantaneous angle between their paths, and offer a distractor that neglects the transverse component of the target's motion when calculating the centripetal acceleration.
example_sketch: A heat-seeking missile flies at a constant speed of three hundred meters per second, always pointing directly at a target drone that is cruising horizontally at two hundred meters per second. When the line of sight to the drone is perpendicular to the drone's velocity and the distance is one kilometer, the missile undergoes a purely lateral centripetal acceleration. This acceleration is determined solely by the angular rotation rate of the line of sight caused by the drone's motion.

name: curvature-preserving-kinematic-scaling
statement: Finding the peak acceleration of an agent traversing a scaled-down geometric trajectory at a constant speed requires mapping the minimum radius of curvature of the original unscaled physical trajectory to the model scale.
why_it_bites: It punishes the confusion between the physical acceleration governing the original trajectory (e.g., gravity) and the purely geometric curvature of the path, as well as the incorrect scaling of curvature which has dimensions of inverse length.
trap_recipe: Provide a physical trajectory under gravity and a scale factor, then ask for the acceleration of an insect tracing the model path at constant speed; construct a distractor where the acceleration is simply scaled linearly with the scale factor or remains equal to gravity.
example_sketch: A roller coaster loop is plotted on a blueprint at a 1:50 scale. A toy car travels along this blueprint path at a constant speed of 0.5 m/s. To find the maximum acceleration experienced by the toy car, one must find the minimum radius of curvature of the real coaster's parabolic path and scale it down to compute the centripetal acceleration.
signals: uniform speed along a curved path
maximum acceleration of an insect/particle on a graph

name: relative-frame-odometry-coupling
statement: When an agent moves across sequential moving substrates, onboard instruments measure motion relative to the local substrate, while spatial boundary conditions and transit times are constrained by the laboratory frame.
why_it_bites: It exploits the tendency to add laboratory displacements and relative displacements without accounting for how the shifting frame transforms the time spent on each segment.
trap_recipe: Construct a scenario where the total odometer reading equals the physical length of the track, tempting the student to write a simple distance sum in the laboratory frame, which fails because the odometer registers the moving substrate's extra displacement.
example_sketch: A surveyor's wheel is rolled across two successive airport moving walkways of equal length moving at different speeds in the same direction. The total distance registered by the wheel's odometer equals the physical length of one walkway. One must determine the wheel's speed relative to each walkway given the total travel time.

name: projection-invariance-pure-pursuit
statement: In a 2D pure pursuit where a chaser's velocity always points toward a target moving in a straight line, the equation of motion is integrated by projecting the velocities onto the instantaneous line of sight rather than solving the spiral trajectory coordinates.
why_it_bites: It punishes students who attempt to write down and solve the non-linear differential equations of the actual 2D trajectory in Cartesian coordinates, which quickly becomes intractable.
trap_recipe: Provide the initial separation and velocities, and ask for the capture time. Create a distractor based on a simple straight-line right-triangle path using the Pythagorean theorem statically, or one using simple relative velocity along the initial line of sight.
example_sketch: A heat-seeking missile with constant speed v locks onto a jet flying in a straight line at constant speed u (where u < v). If the missile starts at a right angle to the jet's path at a distance d, finding the time of impact relies on integrating the projection of their relative velocity along the line of sight.

name: signal-propagation-compression
statement: An observer measuring the speed of an approaching or receding object via periodic pulses experiences an apparent velocity that differs from the true velocity because the signal propagation delay changes continuously with distance.
why_it_bites: It traps students who apply standard kinematic definitions using the observer's arrival time interval instead of the source's emission time interval, or who mistakenly think relativistic Doppler effects are required for any apparent speed shift.
trap_recipe: Ask for the 'measured speed' of an incoming object emitting sound/light pulses. Include a distractor that is simply the projection of the true velocity along the line of sight, or one that uses the standard Doppler frequency shift formula inverted incorrectly.
example_sketch: A high-speed train travels along a straight track at a constant fraction of the speed of sound, emitting a whistle flash at regular intervals. A stationary observer standing ahead of the track records the time of arrivals of the sound waves to calculate the train's speed. To find the true speed from the recorded profile, the changing acoustic transit time must be deconvolved from the emission intervals.

name: thickness-regulated-spiral-accumulation
statement: The kinematics of winding a thin sheet onto a spool is solved by equating the conserved cross-sectional area of the wound roll to the product of the sheet's thickness and its total unrolled length.
why_it_bites: It punishes students who attempt to set up a grueling spiral arc-length integral with a changing radius, which is mathematically tedious and highly prone to algebraic errors.
trap_recipe: Provide the initial radius, final outer radius, and total winding time, then ask for the length wound at a fraction of that time. The naive trap is to assume the radius or wound length increases linearly with time.
example_sketch: A roll of paper towel with a known inner and outer radius is wound onto a cardboard tube at a constant angular speed. To find how much paper has been fed onto the roll after a certain time, one must utilize the conservation of the circular cross-sectional area of the roll rather than integrating the spiral path.

name: optimal-vector-velocity-transition
statement: To transition a velocity vector to another target velocity vector in the minimum possible time using a constant magnitude of acceleration, the acceleration vector must maintain a constant direction pointing along the vector difference between the initial and final velocities.
why_it_bites: It punishes the intuition that a smooth, curved path (like a circular arc where acceleration is always perpendicular to velocity) is the fastest way to turn, or that acceleration must be split into independent orthogonal components solved sequentially.
trap_recipe: Design a maneuver where a vehicle must change its velocity from due East to due North. Offer distractors calculated using a constant-radius circular turn or sequential deceleration and acceleration phases.
example_sketch: A drone cruising due East at a constant speed must change its flight path to travel due North at the same speed. To minimize the transition time under a fixed engine thrust limit, the drone must apply a constant acceleration pointing Northwest, resulting in a parabolic trajectory rather than a circular bank.

name: propagation-delay-dilation
statement: The observed duration of a continuous signal or pulse emitted by a moving source differs from its emission duration due to the continuously changing propagation distance (and hence travel time) between the source and the receiver during the emission window.
why_it_bites: It punishes the assumption that because wave speed is constant in the medium, the duration of a signal is invariant under classical Galilean relativity, or the misapplication of frequency-only Doppler shift formulas to finite-time intervals.
trap_recipe: An emitter travels towards a stationary receiver at speed v and emits a pulse of duration T. Ask for the received pulse duration, and provide options of exactly T or options that apply relativistic time dilation instead of classical acoustic propagation delay.
example_sketch: An acoustic beacon moving underwater towards a hydrophone array emits a constant sonar ping lasting exactly 5 seconds. Because the beacon is closer to the receiver when it finishes emitting than when it started, the hydrophone records a compressed ping lasting fewer than 5 seconds.

name: boundary-projection-matching
statement: For a receiver moving parallel to a reflecting surface to continuously detect a wave reflected from its own emitter, the projection of the receiver's velocity along the boundary must match the projection of the reflected wave's phase velocity along that same boundary.
why_it_bites: Students try to solve the problem by tracking individual ray paths and calculating dynamic angles of incidence, failing to realize that the boundary-matching condition simplifies the relative velocity vectors into a single 1D projection constraint.
trap_recipe: Set up a scenario with an observer moving parallel to a wall who emits a continuous wave. Ask for the condition to hear the echo. Offer incorrect options that match the total speeds directly or use normal incidence angles instead of tangential projection components.
example_sketch: A high-speed train travels parallel to a long concrete retaining wall while sounding its horn. To determine the angle at which the reflected sound waves must return to continuously meet the moving train, one must equate the train's speed to the parallel component of the sound wave's velocity along the wall.

name: symmetric-relative-projection
statement: If an observer's relative speed is identical with respect to two collinear objects moving along a single line, the projection of the observer's velocity vector onto that line must equal the arithmetic mean of the two objects' velocities.
why_it_bites: It exploits the tendency to set up complex 2D systems of trigonometric equations for relative motion vectors, whereas the symmetry of relative speeds directly collapses the problem to a one-dimensional projection average.
trap_recipe: Position two targets moving at different speeds along a straight highway, and place an observer on an intersecting angled path such that they maintain equal relative speeds to both targets. Provide options that use geometric averages of the speeds or ignore the cosine of the intersection angle.
example_sketch: Two automated carts travel along a straight track at different constant speeds. An inspector walks along a straight diagonal path crossing the tracks in such a way that their relative speed to both carts is always identical; this requires the projection of the inspector's velocity along the track to be the average of the two cart speeds.

name: similar-triangle-force-bijection
statement: When a particle is in static equilibrium on a spherical surface under the action of gravity, a normal contact force, and a tension force directed toward a fixed point, the vector triangle of forces is geometrically similar to the physical triangle formed by the contact point, the center of curvature, and the tension anchor.
why_it_bites: It punishes the tedious approach of projecting forces onto local coordinates (which leads to complex trigonometric relations and differential calculus) by offering a clean, constant-ratio geometric shortcut.
trap_recipe: Ask how the normal contact force changes as a bead is slowly pulled up a sphere by a rope threaded through a pulley at the top. Make the 'intuitive' wrong options suggest that the normal force increases or decreases as the bead gets closer to the apex, whereas the geometric similarity proves it remains completely constant.
example_sketch: A heavy bead is slowly pulled up a frictionless spherical dome by a light string connected to a winch located at the very top of the dome. As the bead rises, the tension in the string changes, but the normal force exerted by the dome on the bead remains perfectly constant.

name: target-frame-chase-transformation
statement: A pursuit problem where an agent constantly steers toward a moving target can be simplified by analyzing the motion in a reference frame translating with the target's velocity, transforming the target into a stationary point and the agent's steering into a vector field overlaid with a constant virtual wind.
why_it_bites: Solving the equations of pursuit in the lab frame results in non-linear, coupled differential equations that are mathematically intractable for students without advanced calculus.
trap_recipe: Set up a chase scenario (e.g., a boat chasing a drifting raft) and ask for the time of capture. Provide formulaic options that incorrectly assume linear relative velocity or ignore the changing angle of pursuit.
example_sketch: A guided missile always steers directly toward a drone flying at a constant velocity perpendicular to the missile's initial line of sight. By transforming to the drone's frame of reference, the drone becomes stationary while a constant virtual wind acts on the missile, allowing the collision time to be solved using simple vector components.

name: tension-continuity-geometric-constraint
statement: A frictionless pulley free to slide on a continuous rope suspended between two supports must distribute tension equally along both segments, geometrically forcing the segments to make equal angles with the horizontal regardless of any vertical height differences between the supports.
why_it_bites: It violates the strong visual intuition that the pulley will slide towards the lower support or that asymmetric support heights must create asymmetric angles and tensions at equilibrium.
trap_recipe: Hang a pulley from a string tied to two pegs at wildly different heights. Ask for the ratio of the tensions in the left and right segments of the string, offering options proportional to the heights of the pegs or the lengths of the string segments.
example_sketch: A heavy ring slides without friction along a slack clothesline tied between a low fence post and a tall tree branch. When a weight is hung from the ring and it settles, the two halves of the clothesline adjust themselves to make identical angles with the horizontal.

name: circle-of-least-time
statement: In a uniform gravitational field, the locus of points reachable from a common origin in a given time via frictionless straight tracks is a circle tangent to the horizontal starting plane, meaning the path of quickest descent to a destination line is determined by the chord of a circle tangent to that line.
why_it_bites: Students mistakenly optimize for the shortest distance (the perpendicular path to the target line) or the maximum acceleration path (straight vertical), neglecting how the trade-off between path length and acceleration varies with the track angle.
trap_recipe: Ask for the optimal slope of a slide designed to get a block from an elevated point to a tilted ramp in the minimum time. Include the perpendicular distance path and the path of steepest descent as highly attractive distractor options.
example_sketch: A wire is to be stretched from a high ceiling hook to a long, inclined metal ramp. To find the path that allows a sliding bead to reach the ramp in the shortest possible time, one must construct a circle passing through the hook that is tangent to the ramp.

name: quasistatic-spring-force-invariance
statement: When a massless spring is pulled quasistatically by a force, its extension is determined solely by that force and its spring constant, regardless of the boundary condition or acceleration of the object attached to its other end.
why_it_bites: It punishes the misconception that a spring attached to an accelerating mass or a sliding mass under friction transmits less force (and thus stretches less) than one attached to a rigid wall.
trap_recipe: Present three scenarios where a spring is pulled by the same slowly increasing force: one anchored to a wall, one attached to a block on a rough floor that does not slip, and one attached to a block sliding on a frictionless floor. Ask the student to compare the final extensions, and make 'extension is largest when anchored to the wall' the highly tempting incorrect choice.
example_sketch: A force sensor pulls a light spring of constant k with a slowly ramping force up to 10 N. In Case A, the other end of the spring is fixed to a rigid wall; in Case B, it is attached to a 5 kg block on a smooth sheet of ice. The ratio of the spring's extension in Case A to Case B is exactly 1:1 because the massless spring remains in instantaneous equilibrium during the slow ramp.

name: internal-node-elastic-splitting
statement: Applying a force to an internal point of a stretched elastic line divides it into two parallel-acting spring segments whose combined effective stiffness is the sum of the individual stiffnesses of the two segments.
why_it_bites: It exploits the tendency to use the overall spring constant directly, or to assume that the relaxed length of the spring must be known to compute local displacements.
trap_recipe: Specify an overall spring of stiffness K stretched between two fixed supports, and ask for the displacement of a point at a fractional distance along its length when pulled parallel to its axis by a force F. Offer the simple option 'cannot be determined without the relaxed length' alongside distractors using K directly instead of the parallel combination of the two segments.
example_sketch: A rubber band of stiffness K is stretched between two posts. A tiny bead is clamped to a point one-third of the way along its length and pulled parallel to the band with a force F. The displacement of the bead is found by treating the two segments as parallel springs of stiffness 3K and 1.5K, yielding an effective stiffness of 4.5K.
signals: point on the spring is pulled

name: wedge-slip-critical-angle
statement: An object squeezed between two converging boundaries will slip outward unless the coefficient of static friction is greater than the trigonometric tangent of half the convergence angle.
why_it_bites: It counters the intuition that squeezing harder can always prevent an object from slipping, ignoring that both the outward-pushing normal force component and the retaining friction force scale linearly with the squeezing force.
trap_recipe: Ask for the minimum coefficient of friction needed to grip a round peg between two converging jaws. Include 'depends on the force applied to the jaws' as a highly attractive distractor, along with incorrect trigonometric factors of the convergence angle.
example_sketch: A worker tries to hold a smooth cylindrical log between the jaws of a large mechanical claw. If the jaws meet at too wide an angle, the log will shoot forward and slip out of the claw, no matter how much hydraulic pressure is applied to tighten the grip.

name: velocity-inversion-drag-doubling
statement: When an object moving at its terminal velocity under velocity-dependent drag undergoes an instantaneous elastic direction reversal, the drag force instantly flips direction to assist gravity rather than oppose it, resulting in an initial net acceleration of twice the gravitational acceleration.
why_it_bites: It punishes the misconception that elastic collisions preserve acceleration magnitude, or that drag is negligible immediately after a bounce because the speed is the same.
trap_recipe: Ask for the acceleration of a falling body immediately after bouncing elastically off the floor at terminal velocity, and include $g$ (no drag effect) and $0$ (terminal equilibrium maintained) as tempting options.
example_sketch: A light probe descends through a dense planetary atmosphere and reaches a terminal velocity of $10\text{ m/s}$. It then collides elastically with a flat, rigid surface, bouncing straight back up at $10\text{ m/s}$. Immediately after the bounce, the probe's downward acceleration is measured to be $2g$.

name: frame-shift-friction-linearization
statement: When an object slides on a surface moving at a constant velocity, the kinetic friction force changes direction dynamically in the laboratory frame, but in the frame of the moving surface, it acts in a constant direction opposing the initial relative velocity.
why_it_bites: It exploits the tendency of students to write and solve equations of motion in the lab frame, where the changing direction of the friction force leads to intractable non-linear differential equations.
trap_recipe: Design a scenario where a block is launched onto a wide conveyor belt perpendicular to the belt's motion, and ask for the minimum width of the belt to keep the block on it, prompting students to incorrectly treat the two perpendicular axes as decoupled 1D deceleration problems.
example_sketch: A puck is launched at right angles onto a wide conveyor belt running at a steady pace. By analyzing the motion in the reference frame of the belt, the trajectory is simplified to a straight line of constant deceleration, allowing the sliding time and displacement to be found with basic 1D kinematics.

name: centripetal-equivalence-of-curvature
statement: The three-dimensional radius of curvature of a trajectory is determined by the ratio of the square of the total velocity to the perpendicular acceleration, meaning that superimposing a perpendicular constant velocity onto a circular motion increases the radius of curvature.
why_it_bites: It targets the spatial intuition that the radius of curvature of a helical path must equal the radius of the cylinder on which it is wound, forgetting that curvature is a kinematic property relating velocity and centripetal acceleration.
trap_recipe: Ask for the radius of curvature of a particle moving in a helix formed by constant axial translation and uniform circular motion, making the radius of the cylinder a primary distractor.
example_sketch: A charged particle spirals in a uniform magnetic field, tracing a helix with a radius of 5 cm and a pitch equal to its circumference. To find the trajectory's true 3D radius of curvature, one must compute the ratio of the total squared velocity to the constant centripetal acceleration, which yields a value strictly greater than 5 cm.

name: unlabeled-state-space-ratio
statement: When a system's displacement is determined by integrating a velocity-dependent dynamic relation over an interval, any unknown constant scaling factor on the force or acceleration axis cancels out when calculating ratios of displacement between different velocity bounds.
why_it_bites: It punishes the premature conclusion that a mechanics problem lacks sufficient information because a key graphical axis or physical constant is unlabeled, failing to recognize the scale-invariance of the ratio of integrals.
trap_recipe: Provide an uncalibrated graph of drag force versus velocity for a coasting vehicle, state the distance it takes to slow down over one velocity interval, and ask for the distance needed to stop completely, including a 'not enough information' option.
example_sketch: A small sub-surface drone experiences a drag force that varies quadratically with its speed, plotted on a graph with an unlabeled vertical axis. If the drone glides from 4 m/s to 2 m/s over a distance of 12 meters, the further distance required to slow from 2 m/s to 1 m/s can be found without knowing the density of the fluid or the cross-sectional area.

name: dynamic-tension-reduction
statement: A flexible heavy cord sliding off a support experiences a dynamic tension profile that is modified by its acceleration, reaching its maximum value at an intermediate state rather than when it hangs fully vertical.
why_it_bites: It punishes the assumption that tension in a hanging cord is always statically determined by the weight of the hanging portion, ignoring the reduction in tension caused by the system's downward acceleration.
trap_recipe: Ask for the maximum length of a sliding rope that won't break, and offer a distractor calculated by setting the static tension of the fully hanging rope equal to the breaking strength, which misses the quadratic peak of dynamic tension at the half-way point.
example_sketch: A heavy uniform cable is laid on a smooth table with a small fraction hanging over. Once released, the cable slides off; the maximum tension anywhere in the cable peak is reached when exactly half of its length has slid off the edge, rather than at the very end of the motion.

name: massless-interface-friction-matching
statement: When a massless sheet is sandwiched between two sliding bodies, the friction forces on its opposite faces must be exactly equal in magnitude to maintain a finite acceleration of the sheet.
why_it_bites: It punishes the mistake of applying independent maximum static/kinetic friction limits to both sides of a thin interface without enforcing the zero-net-force constraint required by a massless mediator.
trap_recipe: Provide different friction coefficients for the top and bottom of a paper sheet between a block and an incline, and create distractors where the sheet accelerates independently because the student failed to clamp both friction forces to the lesser of the two thresholds.
example_sketch: A thin massless ribbon is placed between a heavy brick and a conveyor belt. If the ribbon is pulled, the tension in the ribbon is limited strictly by the smaller of the two friction coefficients of its interfaces, regardless of how hard the other surface grips.

name: accelerating-spring-tension-gradient
statement: In an accelerated massive or segmented elastic system pulled from one end, the internal tension decreases linearly toward the free end, causing failure or extension to localise near the pulled end.
why_it_bites: It exploits the misconception that tension is uniform throughout a stretched spring, which is only true for static, massless springs.
trap_recipe: Ask for the total extension of a glued spring being accelerated on a floor before it breaks, and design a distractor assuming the entire spring experiences the maximum pulling force, rather than integrating the linearly decreasing tension along its length.
example_sketch: A train of identical blocks connected by fragile wax seals is pulled across ice from the front block. Because the tension required to drag the remaining train decreases with each block, only the first few wax seals closest to the puller break, while the rear seals remain completely intact.
signals: pulled from one of its ends

name: zero-relaxed-length-linearity
statement: An elastic cord with a relaxed length of zero produces a tension vector that is directly proportional to the position vector, allowing multi-dimensional motion to be decoupled into independent linear coordinate equations.
why_it_bites: It traps students into performing tedious trigonometric resolutions and path integrations because they fail to recognize that the vector force equation simplifies to a decoupled linear system.
trap_recipe: Ask for the path of a mass crawling along a zero-relaxed-length spring suspended between two walls, and provide distractors containing complex transcendental functions (like catenaries or ellipses) instead of the simple linear trajectory yielded by coordinate decoupling.
example_sketch: A heavy bead is free to slide along a vertical hoop while attached to a fixed hook at the top by a spring of zero relaxed length. By decomposing the spring force into Cartesian coordinates, the vertical and horizontal components of the motion decouple, simplifying the equilibrium analysis.

name: unilateral-constraint-slackening
statement: In systems with pre-tensioned, one-way elastic supports, sufficient external acceleration will cause some supports to go slack, abruptly changing the system's effective stiffness and governing equations.
why_it_bites: It punishes the blind application of a symmetric linear system of equations across all acceleration ranges, forgetting to check if any of the elastic elements have transitioned from tension to compression (which they cannot support).
trap_recipe: Ask for the tension in a multi-spring suspension system under high acceleration, and offer a distractor that assumes the compressed springs continue to pull with a negative tension.
example_sketch: A heavy crate is suspended inside a shipping container by pre-tensioned bungee cords secured to the ceiling and floor. When the container undergoes a sudden upward acceleration, the bottom cords go completely slack, instantly dropping the system's resonant frequency as only the top cords remain active.

name: spatially-varying-sliding-thresholds
statement: On an inclined rotating surface, the boundary separating slipping and non-slipping static particles is defined by the locus where the vector sum of the constant gravitational component parallel to the plane and the radial centrifugal force equals the maximum static friction.
why_it_bites: It punishes the assumption of a homogeneous threshold, where students treat a distributed mass layer as a single point mass or assume the entire surface begins to slip simultaneously.
trap_recipe: Calculate the critical angular velocity using the center of mass of the distributed layer, or evaluate the threshold only at the outermost radius, creating a binary all-or-nothing slip transition.
example_sketch: A flat, tilted turntable is evenly coated with a fine layer of static-sensitive micro-beads. As the turntable is slowly spun up, the beads at the outer edges slide off first, but only within a cardioid-like angular sector where gravity and centrifugal forces reinforce each other. We want to find the rotation speed at which exactly half the surface area of the turntable is cleared.

name: curvature-centripetal-coupling-in-3d
statement: For motion constrained to a three-dimensional curved path, the total normal force determining kinetic friction must vectorially combine the static gravitational constraint force and the dynamic centripetal reaction force arising from the path's local curvature.
why_it_bites: It punishes the habit of calculating normal force purely from the static gravity component perpendicular to the path, neglecting the speed-dependent constraint forces required to keep the particle on a curved trajectory.
trap_recipe: Formulate the friction force using only the static normal force (e.g., $N = mg \cos\theta$), completely omitting the $mv^2/R$ centripetal component from the normal force magnitude.
example_sketch: A small bead is launched with speed $v$ down a vertical spiral slide of constant radius $R$ and slope angle $\beta$. To find the terminal speed of the bead, one must balance gravity not just against a constant friction force, but against a friction force that grows with the square of the speed due to the slide's horizontal curvature.

name: instantaneous-centripetal-tension-jump
statement: When a constrained rigid system at rest is instantaneously given a non-zero velocity without any initial displacement, the support tensions must undergo a discontinuous step-change to provide the required centripetal accelerations dictated by the new velocity state.
why_it_bites: It punishes the misconception that forces in inextensible supports only change once the physical positions or angles of those supports change.
trap_recipe: Calculate the tension immediately after the velocity is imparted using the static equilibrium equations, leading to an incorrect result of zero instantaneous tension change.
example_sketch: A heavy horizontal rod is suspended from the ceiling by two vertical, parallel elastic bands. Suddenly, the rod is struck horizontally such that it begins to spin about its center with angular velocity $\omega$. Find the instantaneous change in the tension of the bands immediately after the impact, before any tilt or displacement occurs.

name: friction-gravity-hodograph-alignment
statement: When a particle slides on a 2D inclined plane under the influence of constant gravity and a kinetic friction force that is always anti-parallel to the velocity vector, the equations of motion are non-linearly coupled through the direction of the velocity vector.
why_it_bites: It punishes the assumption that orthogonal components of motion can be analyzed independently when kinetic friction is present.
trap_recipe: Decompose the kinetic friction force into independent constant Cartesian components, or assume the direction of friction remains aligned with the initial velocity vector.
example_sketch: A hockey puck is launched sideways across a steep, icy hill where kinetic friction is isotropic. Because the puck's velocity vector bends downward under gravity, the friction force dynamically rotates, requiring a coordinate-free integration of the velocity angle to find the exact stopping point.
signals: given a velocity at an angle

name: inertia-locked-initial-geometry
statement: Immediately after a system is released from rest, the positions of all finite-mass bodies are temporarily frozen at their initial coordinates, meaning all geometric angles and constraint directions remain identical to their static configuration at $t = 0^+$, even though accelerations and forces jump discontinuously.
why_it_bites: It punishes the instinct to analyze the initial state using the kinematics of the subsequent motion, or assuming that because a constraint (like a string or normal interface) will tilt or slacken later, it must do so immediately at $t = 0^+$.
trap_recipe: Set up a heavy block suspended by a vertical cord from a movable cart. Release the cart from rest. The trap is to assume the cord immediately swings to an angle relative to the vertical to calculate the initial acceleration, rather than keeping the cord perfectly vertical at $t = 0^+$.
example_sketch: A heavy block rests on a horizontal platform while suspended by a vertical string from a cart. If the platform is suddenly dropped, the block's initial acceleration is purely vertical because its inertia prevents instantaneous horizontal displacement, keeping the string perfectly vertical at $t=0^+$ despite the cart being free to move.
signals: connected by a string/rod to a movable body

name: force-modulated-normal-friction
statement: When an applied force has a component normal to the contact interface, changing the magnitude of the pull simultaneously scales the maximum allowable static friction, creating a coupling where the threshold of slipping is a dynamic function of the pulling force itself.
why_it_bites: It punishes the standard workflow of calculating a static friction threshold ($f_{max} = \mu N$) using only the gravitational weight, neglecting the fact that the tension or applied force itself changes $N$, making the slip condition a coupled algebraic equation.
trap_recipe: Design a system where a rope pulls a block at an angle that increases the clamping force against a wall. Ask for the minimum force to cause slip. A student will calculate $f_{max}$ using the $F=0$ normal force, failing to write the threshold as a function of the unknown force $F$.
example_sketch: A heavy wedge is squeezed into a V-shaped groove by an adjustable downward force $F$, while a horizontal pull $P$ attempts to slide the wedge along the groove. Because $F$ directly dictates the normal force on the groove's sloped walls, the threshold of horizontal sliding scales linearly with $F$, forcing a coupled multi-variable static equilibrium analysis.
signals: normal reaction depends on the applied force

name: competing-decay-stopping-conditions
statement: For a moving body undergoing both deceleration and continuous mass loss, the motion's termination is governed by a race between velocity reaching zero and mass reaching zero, leading to a piecewise-defined stopping condition.
why_it_bites: It punishes the blind integration of a differential equation without checking physical boundaries, leading students to calculate non-physical negative masses or continuing to apply friction equations to a body that has already entirely ceased to exist.
trap_recipe: Design a block of dry ice sliding on a rough floor, where both the sublimation rate (mass loss) and deceleration (friction) are known. Ask for the distance traveled. Provide parameters such that the block completely sublimates before it stops due to friction, but the formula for velocity-stop gives a different, longer distance.
example_sketch: A block of soft chalk is pushed across a rough blackboard, wearing away at a rate proportional to its velocity while decelerating under kinetic friction. To find where it leaves its final mark, one must determine whether the chalk's mass is entirely ground to dust before its forward velocity is brought to zero by the friction force.

name: impulse-drag-vector-flip
statement: When a moving projectile experiences velocity-dependent drag, an instantaneous velocity change (impulse) instantly rotates the drag force vector discontinuously, drastically altering the net acceleration vector immediately after the impulse.
why_it_bites: Students often assume drag acts as a continuous history-dependent force, or they forget that the drag vector must instantly realign with the new velocity vector post-impulse, leading them to add the pre-impulse and post-impulse forces incorrectly.
trap_recipe: A ball is falling vertically at terminal velocity under quadratic drag. It is struck horizontally, instantly giving it a horizontal velocity component equal to its vertical terminal velocity. The trap is to assume the vertical drag remains equal to gravity immediately after the hit, neglecting that the speed has increased and the velocity vector has tilted, which increases the total drag and introduces a vertical deceleration.
example_sketch: A projectile is falling vertically at terminal velocity through a dense fluid when it is suddenly struck by a horizontal gust of wind that imparts an instantaneous horizontal velocity. Immediately after the impulse, the total speed increases, which scales up the magnitude of the drag force and tilts its direction, causing the vertical acceleration to suddenly jump from zero to a non-zero upward value.

name: galilean-invariance-relative-displacement
statement: When analyzing the energy expended to travel a fixed distance relative to a uniformly drifting medium, transforming to the co-moving frame of the medium reveals that the energy required to reach any point on a circle of radius R centered on a co-drifting target is isotropic and independent of the drift speed.
why_it_bites: It punishes the instinct to perform vector additions of velocities in the ground frame and integrate power over time, which leads to the false conclusion that traveling upstream or cross-stream requires different amounts of energy relative to the water.
trap_recipe: Design a scenario where a self-propelled vehicle must reach a target floating in a current, and ask to compare the energy needed for different paths (with/against/across the current). Include seductive options claiming that downstream paths require less energy or that the answer depends on the observer's frame.
example_sketch: A swimmer in a wide, steadily flowing river wants to retrieve a floating ball that is currently 50 meters away. Whether the swimmer swims 50 meters directly upstream to get it, 50 meters directly downstream, or 50 meters perpendicular to the current, the total metabolic energy expended is identical because the physics is invariant in the water's frame.
signals: energy expended to reach a relative distance

name: normal-force-friction-asymmetry
statement: When an applied force has a vertical component, pushing down increases the normal force and dry friction, while pulling up decreases them, making the work required to displace the object over a rough surface asymmetric under force reflection.
why_it_bites: It exploits the over-simplification that equal force magnitudes applied over the same displacement must do equal work, neglecting the coupling between the vertical component of the applied force and the normal force.
trap_recipe: Ask for the ratio of work done to move a block a distance d by pushing at angle theta versus pulling at angle theta with the same magnitude of force, or state that the forces are equal and prompt for a comparison of work. Place 'equal work' as a highly attractive distractor.
example_sketch: A warehouse worker must drag a heavy crate 10 meters across a high-friction concrete floor using a rope tilted at 30 degrees above the horizontal. If he flips the rope to push the crate at 30 degrees below the horizontal with the same force magnitude, the normal force increases, causing the friction force to spike and requiring significantly more work to achieve the same displacement.

name: buoyant-work-neutralization
statement: For a fully submerged object with density matching the fluid, no net external work is required to translate it vertically within the fluid because buoyancy perfectly cancels gravity; work is only performed against gravity during the partial emergence phase as buoyant force dynamically decreases.
why_it_bites: Students frequently calculate work as the total gravitational potential energy change (mgd) or use the average force over the entire displacement, forgetting that the net force is zero until the object begins to breach the surface.
trap_recipe: Create a problem where a neutral or near-neutral buoyancy object is raised from a deep reservoir of depth d to the surface. Offer mgd or mg(d - l/2) as bait, when the actual work only depends on the length of the object emerging, not the depth of the reservoir.
example_sketch: A neutral-buoyancy stone cylinder of height 2 meters rests at the bottom of a 50-meter-deep lake. To pull it completely out of the water, a crane does zero work over the first 48 meters of ascent, with all non-zero work occurring during the final 2 meters as the buoyant support progressively vanishes.

name: sudden-parameter-modulation-oscillation-center
statement: Abruptly removing a constant field (like gravity) from a spring-mass system does not instantly change the position or momentum of the mass, but instantaneously shifts the equilibrium position, converting the instantaneous displacement from the new equilibrium into a new amplitude.
why_it_bites: Students often think that switching off gravity at the lowest point (maximum spring stretch) causes the system to stop oscillating, or that the amplitude remains unchanged but the frequency shifts, or they confuse the new center of oscillation.
trap_recipe: Ask for the new amplitude or the plot of motion after gravity is abruptly turned off at an extreme of the motion (e.g., the lowest point). Provide options showing the oscillation centered around the old equilibrium or decaying to zero.
example_sketch: A mass hanging from a vertical spring is oscillating when gravity is suddenly 'turned off' at the exact moment the mass reaches its lowest point. Because the spring's unstretched position is the new equilibrium, and the mass is currently far from this position with zero velocity, it begins a new oscillation with a larger amplitude centered on the unstretched spring position.

name: path-independent-friction-work
statement: For a particle sliding slowly along a curved track of arbitrary vertical profile under gravity, if its speed is low enough that centripetal acceleration is negligible, the work done by friction is solely proportional to the horizontal projection of its displacement.
why_it_bites: Students assume they need the detailed path profile to calculate the line integral of friction, or they believe the vertical profile of the path dictates the energy lost to friction.
trap_recipe: Provide a highly complex, multi-humped curve with a specified total horizontal span and vertical drop, along with distracting curve-fitting parameters, then ask for the final speed or stopping point.
example_sketch: A sled slides down a bumpy, irregular hill of total horizontal length 50 m and vertical drop 10 m with a constant coefficient of friction 0.1. Assuming the sled's speed is low enough that vertical accelerations are negligible, we can find its final speed using only the horizontal and vertical displacements. A student trying to integrate over the sinusoidal bumps will get bogged down.

name: moving-constraint-frame-shift
statement: The work done by a frictionless, moving constraint on a particle is best calculated by transforming to the frame where the constraint is stationary to find the final velocity, then transforming back to the lab frame to find the kinetic energy change.
why_it_bites: Students wrongly believe that frictionless boundaries never do work, forgetting this only applies in frames where the boundary is stationary, or they try to integrate a time-varying normal force vector in the lab frame.
trap_recipe: Present a particle interacting with a moving curved wedge or channel that is frictionless and moves at constant velocity. Ask for the work done by the channel on the particle, offering 'zero' as a prominent trap option.
example_sketch: A frictionless spiral tube is mounted on a cart that moves horizontally at a constant speed u. A bead, initially at rest in the lab frame, enters the tube and emerges vertically. By analyzing the motion in the cart's frame where energy is conserved, the exit velocity and thus the work done by the tube in the lab frame can be easily determined.
signals: suddenly made to move with constant velocity

name: unwinding-string-non-conservation
statement: For a mass attached to a string wrapping around a fixed cylinder on a horizontal plane, the tension does no work and speed remains constant, but angular momentum about the cylinder's center is not conserved because the tension force is tangent to the cylinder and exerts a torque.
why_it_bites: Students often try to conserve angular momentum about the cylinder's central axis, wrongly concluding that the speed must increase as the radius decreases.
trap_recipe: Ask for the speed and tension of a particle on a wrapping string after the free length has halved, and offer a choice where speed increases by a factor of 2 and tension increases by a factor of 8.
example_sketch: A puck on an ice rink is attached to a peg by a string and is projected perpendicularly, causing the string to wrap around the peg. Because the instantaneous velocity is always perpendicular to the free segment of the string, the speed of the puck remains constant while the tension increases as the free radius of curvature shrinks.

name: distance-averaged-drag-work
statement: When a particle reaches a terminal velocity under a velocity-dependent drag force, the average drag force over the distance traveled is determined directly via the work-energy theorem, whereas the time-average force is simply equal to the gravitational force.
why_it_bites: Students confuse distance-averaged force with time-averaged force, or get stuck trying to integrate the exponential velocity approach to terminal velocity over distance.
trap_recipe: Ask for the average viscous drag force over a fall of height h that ends at terminal velocity, and include the time-average option (mg) and incorrect factors of 1/2 from misapplying linear average approximations.
example_sketch: A small bead is dropped from rest in a viscous fluid and reaches its terminal velocity after falling a total distance H. The average drag force acting on the bead over this distance can be found using the work-energy theorem without needing the explicit velocity profile.

name: constant-velocity-carrier-dissipation
statement: When an object is accelerated from rest to match the constant speed of a moving carrier via a constant friction force, the external work required to maintain the carrier's speed is exactly twice the final kinetic energy of the synchronized object.
why_it_bites: It punishes the naive application of the work-energy theorem on the accelerated object alone, where students forget that the displacement of the carrier (where the external force acts) is exactly twice the displacement of the slipping object.
trap_recipe: Ask for the total work done by a conveyor belt's motor to bring a dropped package up to speed, and list the package's final kinetic energy as the primary distractor.
example_sketch: A package of mass $m$ is dropped vertically onto a horizontal luggage carousel rotating at a constant tangential speed $v$. Before it stops slipping, the carousel's motor must perform a specific amount of work to maintain its speed. This work is exactly double the kinetic energy eventually acquired by the package.

name: static-kinetic-friction-hysteresis-stroke
statement: In a spring-mass system on a rough surface that is loaded until slip begins, the initial release threshold is determined by static friction, while the subsequent single-stroke slide and stopping position are determined by kinetic friction.
why_it_bites: It punishes the failure to separate static friction (which defines the initial spring energy boundary condition) from kinetic friction (which governs the energy dissipation during active motion).
trap_recipe: Provide the spring constant and the distance of a single slip stroke, then ask for the static friction coefficient. Ensure the trap answer incorrectly uses kinetic friction to find the starting point or static friction to calculate the work dissipated.
example_sketch: A block resting on a rough floor is attached to a wall by a horizontal spring. The block is slowly nudged away from the wall, stretching the spring until the block suddenly slips and then comes to rest after sliding a distance $d$. By measuring $d$ and knowing the kinetic friction coefficient, one can determine the static friction coefficient.

name: transition-boundary-contact-loss
statement: When a particle slides along a composite curvilinear track, the point of contact loss often occurs not at the peak of a curve, but at transition boundaries or inflection points where the normal force abruptly drops due to a change in the track's radius of curvature.
why_it_bites: It punishes the habit of checking for contact loss ($N = 0$) only at the absolute summits of convex hills, ignoring sudden transitions where curvature changes sign or magnitude.
trap_recipe: Construct a track consisting of a concave arc meeting a convex arc at an angle. Ask for the minimum release height to lose contact, and set the trap answer to correspond to the apex of the convex arc rather than the transition boundary where the normal force instantly vanishes.
example_sketch: A small bead slides down a track made of two circular segments of radius $R$ joined smoothly at an angle of $30^\circ$ from the vertical. The first segment is concave and the second is convex. If the bead is released from too high up, it will fly off the track at the exact boundary where the two segments meet.

name: variable-mass-termination-threshold
statement: When a projectile lifting a flexible chain reaches a height greater than the chain's total length, the system transitions from a variable-mass inelastic phase to a constant-mass free-fall phase, creating a discontinuity in the governing equations of motion.
why_it_bites: It punishes the blind application of a single differential equation or conservation law across the entire trajectory, as students fail to partition the motion into the inelastic chain-lifting phase and the subsequent rigid-body ballistic phase.
trap_recipe: Ask for the maximum height reached by a projectile lifting a chain, and provide a trap choice calculated by assuming the chain has infinite length (continuous variable mass) or by assuming mechanical energy is conserved during the lifting phase.
example_sketch: A heavy anchor is launched vertically from the deck of a ship with an initial velocity, dragging a heavy metal chain of finite length behind it. Once the chain is fully airborne, it no longer accumulates mass but continues to fly ballistically. Determine the maximum height the anchor reaches, accounting for the energy dissipated as each chain link is jerked into motion.

name: unilateral-constraint-detachment
statement: In a multi-body system constrained by a unilateral boundary, the point of detachment occurs when the contact force drops to zero, which is determined by tracking when the kinematic constraints would otherwise require an attractive boundary force.
why_it_bites: It exploits the misconception that a sliding object remains in contact with a guiding surface until its velocity direction physically diverges, rather than when the normal force vanishes due to acceleration constraints.
trap_recipe: Ask for the velocity of a sliding link at the moment of detachment, and design a distractor that assumes the link stays constrained all the way to the end of its geometric track.
example_sketch: A rigid rod of length L stands vertically against a frictionless wall and begins to slide down. As the rod falls, the top end eventually slides off the wall before reaching the floor. Find the angle of the rod at the exact instant it detaches from the wall.

name: elastic-pull-with-localized-friction
statement: When pulling an elastic medium past a localized frictional obstacle, the effective stiffness of the cleared portion scales dynamically with its length, requiring integration of a varying spring force over a path to find total work.
why_it_bites: It punishes the assumption of a constant spring constant, leading students to sum the work done against friction and the final elastic energy as independent, static terms.
trap_recipe: Provide a setup where a stretchy rope is pulled through a tight aperture of constant resistance, and craft a wrong answer that uses the final stretched state stiffness for the entire pulling process.
example_sketch: A bungee cord of unstretched length L is slowly pulled out of a narrow guide hole that exerts a constant friction force F. As more cord is pulled out, the stiffness of the external segment decreases because its relaxed length increases. Find the total work done to pull the entire cord out of the hole.

name: harmonic-deceleration-slippage-limit
statement: When a multi-component system with internal dry friction undergoes simple harmonic motion, the critical threshold for relative sliding is determined by the maximum acceleration of the carrier, which occurs at maximum displacement.
why_it_bites: It preys on the confusion between velocity and acceleration, tempting students to evaluate the slip condition at the moment of maximum velocity or impact, rather than at peak deceleration.
trap_recipe: Provide an initial velocity of a cart hitting a spring with a block on top, and write a distractor that evaluates the slip condition using the initial impact velocity rather than the amplitude of the resulting harmonic motion.
example_sketch: A flatbed truck carrying a heavy crate of mass m strikes a safety spring bumper at speed v. The coefficient of static friction between the crate and the truck bed is mu. Determine the maximum allowable stiffness of the spring buffer such that the crate does not slide relative to the truck bed during deceleration.
signals: strikes the spring with a velocity

name: dissipation-frame-invariance
statement: While individual work terms and kinetic energy changes are frame-dependent, the thermal energy dissipated due to friction or inelastic deformation in a closed system is invariant under Galilean transformations.
why_it_bites: It punishes the misconception that one can compute total heat generated by multiplying friction by the displacement of the sliding object in an arbitrary moving reference frame, rather than using the relative displacement between the sliding surfaces.
trap_recipe: Construct a problem where a block slides on a moving platform. Ask for the heat generated, and provide choices calculated from the work done by friction using the block's displacement in the laboratory frame, or using a naive application of the work-energy theorem in that frame without accounting for the work done on the platform.
example_sketch: A block of mass $m$ is projected with velocity $v$ onto a massive conveyor belt moving at a constant speed $u$ in the opposite direction. After a short period of slipping, the block comes to rest relative to the belt. The total thermal energy generated during this process must be computed using the relative sliding distance, which yields a frame-invariant result unlike the work computed using lab-frame displacements.

name: unwrapping-tension-zero-work
statement: When a flexible, inextensible cord winds or unwinds around a fixed curved obstacle, the instantaneous point of contact of the cord is at rest, meaning the tension force does zero instantaneous work on the moving mass attached to its end.
why_it_bites: It punishes the false assumption that because the path is non-circular and the instantaneous radius of curvature is changing, the tension must do work or the mechanical energy of the mass is not conserved.
trap_recipe: Design a system where a mass on a string unwinds from a fixed cylinder. Ask for the speed of the mass after a certain length of cord has unwound, and provide distractor options where mechanical energy is reduced by a term resembling tension times arc length, or where angular momentum about the cylinder's center is incorrectly assumed to be conserved.
example_sketch: A small puck on a frictionless horizontal table is connected to a fixed peg of radius $R$ by a string of length $L$. The puck is given an initial velocity perpendicular to the string, causing the string to wrap around the peg.
